title: Song of Solomon 7
subtitle: The Dance and the Vineyard

sections.header: Verses 1–9 — The Second Wasf: Feet to Head

heb.paragraph: "Come back, come back, O Shulammite (shullamît)!" (v.1/6:13). The beloved’s only name in the poem. Related to "Solomon" (shəlōmō) and to "peace" (shālōm). She is the feminine counterpart of the king: the peace-woman matching the peace-king.

refs.note: "Husbands ought to love their wives as their own bodies" — the bodily love the wasf celebrates.
"This is now bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh" — the first human celebration of the body that the wasf extends.

notes.note: "How beautiful your sandalled feet, O prince’s daughter! Your graceful legs are like jewels... your navel is a rounded goblet... your waist is a mound of wheat." The upward wasf: from feet to waist.

"Your head crowns you like Mount Carmel. Your hair is like royal tapestry; the king is held captive by its tresses." The wasf culminates at the head. The king is captured by her beauty — power reversed.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: "prince’s daughter" elevates her social status. Whether literally noble or metaphorically elevated by love, she is royalty in the lover’s eyes.
Alter: Mount Carmel crowning her head brings geography into the body. She IS the landscape. The promised land and the beloved are the same.
Calvin: the wasf celebrates the human body as God’s creation. Calvin: the Song’s physicality is not concession to the flesh but honour to the Creator who designed it.
Calvin: the king held captive by love — Calvin: even Solomon’s power yielded to beauty. How much more should the soul yield to Christ’s beauty.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: "prince’s daughter" (bat-nādîb) may be literal (she is noble) or figurative (love ennobles). The ambiguity is the poem’s signature.
The NET: Carmel means "garden land" or "vineyard of God." Her head is the peak of a fertile mountain — beauty and abundance combined.

longman.source: Tremper Longman III, Song of Songs, NICOT (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Longman: the second wasf moves upward — from feet to head — reversing the first (ch 4, head to breasts). Together they create a complete portrait: she is praised from every direction.
Longman: "the king is held captive by its tresses" — the most powerful man in the kingdom is captive to a woman’s hair. Love inverts power hierarchies.

garrett.source: Duane A. Garrett, Song of Songs / Proverbs, NAC (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Garrett: the Shulammite dances (6:13) and the wasf describes what the onlookers see. The description is public — the Daughters of Jerusalem witness. Beauty is communal, not hidden.
Garrett: "held captive" by her hair — the military language continues (cf. 6:4 "majestic as troops"). Love conquers the conqueror.

hist.context: The second wasf describes the beloved from feet upward (the reverse of ch 4’s head-down): sandalled feet, curved thighs, navel like a goblet, waist like a mound of wheat, breasts like fawns, neck like ivory tower, eyes like pools, nose like the tower of Lebanon, head like Carmel, hair that traps a king. The upward direction mirrors her rising: she is ascending in the lover’s vision. "How beautiful your sandalled feet, O prince’s daughter!" (v.1).

sections.header: Verses 10–13 — “I Belong to My Beloved”; To the Vineyards

heb.paragraph: "I belong to my beloved, and his desire (təshūqâ) is for me" (v.10). The final form of the mutual-belonging formula. The progression is complete: "my beloved is mine and I am his" (2:16) → "I am my beloved’s and my beloved is mine" (6:3) → "I belong to my beloved, and his desire is for me" (7:10). The last version is pure surrender and received desire. The word təshūqâ appears only three times in the OT: Gen 3:16 (the woman’s desire for her husband, post-fall), Gen 4:7 (sin’s desire for Cain), and here. The Song redeems the Genesis curse: desire restored to delight.

refs.note: "Your desire will be for your husband" — the curse. Song 7:10 redeems it: desire restored from curse to gift.
"Sin is crouching at your door; its desire is for you" — the only other use of təshūqâ before the Song.
Rachel, Leah, and the mandrakes — the same fertility association.

notes.note: "I belong to my beloved, and his desire is for me." The formula’s final evolution: pure belonging. She no longer claims; she is claimed. His desire directed at her is her identity.
"Come, my beloved, let us go to the countryside... there I will give you my love." The vineyard of forced labour (1:6) becomes the vineyard of chosen intimacy. The love is now given, not compelled.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Alter: the Genesis 3:16 echo is deliberate. The Song places təshūqâ in a redeemed context: desire without domination, longing without curse. What the fall distorted, love restores.
Alter: the mandrakes’ fragrance and the vineyard’s budding connect love to the natural world’s fertility. Human desire and agricultural abundance are not metaphor but shared reality.

Calvin: "his desire is for me" redeems the fall’s distortion of desire. Calvin: in Christ, the curse on sexuality is lifted. Marital desire becomes what God originally intended: mutual delight without dominion.
Calvin: the vineyard invitation is the restored Eden. What was lost in Genesis 3 is glimpsed in the Song: love without shame, desire without curse, intimacy without fear.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: təshūqâ (desire/longing) occurs only in Gen 3:16, 4:7, and here. The intertextual connection with the curse is widely recognised.
The NET: "mandrakes" (dūdāʾam) were associated with fertility and love-magic in the ANE. The root is the same as dōd (beloved). Love-plants for the lover.

longman.source: Tremper Longman III, Song of Songs, NICOT (2001) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Longman: the təshūqâ connection to Genesis 3:16 is the Song’s most theologically charged moment. The curse’s distorted desire is redeemed into the Song’s mutual delight. This is Eden restored in love.

Longman: the vineyard invitation closes a circle: from vineyard labour (1:6) to vineyard love (7:12). What was obligation becomes joy. This is the gospel pattern: what the law demanded, grace enables.

garrett.source: Duane A. Garrett, Song of Songs / Proverbs, NAC (1993) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Garrett: the formula’s progression (2:16 → 6:3 → 7:10) is the poem’s relational arc in miniature. From mutual claiming to mutual belonging to received desire. The relationship matures through the poem.
Garrett: the countryside/vineyard setting returns the poem to pastoral origins. The urban court gives way to rural simplicity. Love’s truest expression is not in the palace but in the garden.

hist.context: The formula reaches its final form: "I belong to my beloved, and his desire is for me." Then the beloved invites: "Come, my beloved, let us go to the countryside... let us go early to the vineyards" (vv.11–12). The vineyard — where she was forced to labour in chapter 1 (v.6) — is now a place of chosen love. "There I will give you my love" (v.12). The mandrakes give off fragrance (v.13) — fertility plants associated with Rachel’s desire for children (Gen 30:14–16). Love, fertility, and the land are united.

ppl.name: Beloved
ppl.text: Beloved is a key figure in The Dance and the Vineyard.

ppl.name: Lover
ppl.text: Lover is a key figure in The Dance and the Vineyard.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVI said, “I will climb the palm tree; I will take hold of its fruit.” May your breasts be like clusters of grapes on the vine, the fragrance of your breath like apples,ESVI say I will climb the palm tree and lay hold of its fruit. Oh may your breasts be like clusters of the vine, and the scent of your breath like apples,NIVYour stature is like that of the palm, and your breasts like clusters of fruit.ESVYour stature is like a palm tree, and your breasts are like its clusters.

rec.text: ""How beautiful your sandalled feet, O prince’s daughter! Your graceful legs are like jewels... your navel is a rounded goblet... your waist is a mound of wheat." The upward wasf: from feet to waist."

rows.label: The Upward Wasf — 7:1–9
rows.text: Feet to head. Prince’s daughter. "The king is held captive by her tresses."

hebtext.note: "Come back, come back, O Shulammite (shullamît)!" (v.1/6:13). The beloved’s only name in the poem. Related to "Solomon" (shəlōmō) and to "peace" (shālōm). She is the feminine counterpart of the king: the peace-woman matching the peace-king.

"I belong to my beloved, and his desire (təshūqâ) is for me" (v.10). The final form of the mutual-belonging formula. The progression is complete: "my beloved is mine and I am his" (2:16) → "I am my beloved’s and my beloved is mine" (6:3) → "I belong to my beloved, and his desire is for me" (7:10). The last version is pure surrender and received desire. The word təshūqâ appears only three times in the OT: Gen 3:16 (the woman’s desire for her husband, post-fall), Gen 4:7 (sin’s desire for Cain), and here. The Song redeems the Genesis curse: desire restored to delight.

thread.text: "Husbands ought to love their wives as their own bodies" — the bodily love the wasf celebrates.
"This is now bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh" — the first human celebration of the body that the wasf extends.
"Your desire will be for your husband" — the curse. Song 7:10 redeems it: desire restored from curse to gift.
"Sin is crouching at your door; its desire is for you" — the only other use of təshūqâ before the Song.
Rachel, Leah, and the mandrakes — the same fertility association.

themes.note: Covenant peaks at 9: the final belonging formula. Prophecy at 8: Genesis 3:16 redeemed — the curse reversed by love. The Song’s theological climax.

prayer_prompt: Come, my beloved, let us go to the countryside. Consider asking God about the invitations you extend to Him — do you invite His presence into your ordinary places?
